speaker: interviewer
text: Number 2, interview with Zalman Finkelstein, August the 15th, 1996. Your house-- one more time I wanted to know. Did you have electricity in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, one bulb.

speaker: interviewer
text: One bulb in only one room, or in every room there was one bulb?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a big room. One, one bulb in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the other rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing, so how did you-- what did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a room and a half. The main room was everything, the kitchen there, the, the [INAUDIBLE], the, the closet, the this, the that, the box, the box where they keep the coal and the wood in it. You know, so this was the big room. And then it was just beds to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that room or in another?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the next.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of you slept in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a little like this maybe, this size.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of you slept in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were sleeping together in the beds. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother and father? Where did they sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: They slept in-- over there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you have running water in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in the hallway, only one faucet--

speaker: interviewer
text: Faucet.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for everybody. It was 10, 12 tenants, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a radio or a telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: A telephone? You're kidding. [LAUGHS] No telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio? Did anybody have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I-- no, I didn't-- I never heard anything, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware-- or what did you know about Hitler and what was going on in Germany and Austria when-- early in the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you-- did Jews from Germany, Polish Jews who lived in Germany, did any of them come to Lodz when-- after Kristallnacht? After--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know nothing about it. I didn't know nothing about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was politics ever discussed in your house at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe they talk between them, and the kids not to hear. But it was-- I had a big brother, and under him, you know, I had a brother. He was already back from the army, finished now in Polish Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe between them. They were talking between them. But, you know, a kid, ah, what you have in your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the fifth child? Yeah. You were the fifth. You were not the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Hershl.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I, I have this. You were five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when you were in Gordonia-- I'm sorry, [NON-ENGLISH], was that discussed there at all, Hitler leaving, making aliyah?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, what they-- yeah. The only thing what they discussed is, is that we should leave here, the places, and run away. And they were talking, and-- but they didn't have the ability to do it. So some people left, I remember. They, they hid-- they hold it a secret. You know, kids may be talking too much or whatever. So people that went to Israel already, I remember this. When they went to Israel, it was secret between the older people. They were going. They were going. Yeah, but they were going illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Illegally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody in your family or friends that went away, not necessarily to Palestine, but to other parts of the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I start being over there, a member. My brother was a member there, not the oldest-- a little one behind him. Never did tell me anything. He knew more. He was already-- I don't know. He was 18, 17? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard-- maybe yes. I don't remember. I don't 'member. How many times I didn't remember my name. I'm not kidding. Some Germans, they [NON-ENGLISH]. What's your name? You know, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did, when did you realize that the Germans were so anti-Semitic and hurting Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember when the war broke out, a lot of Jews start walkin' towards Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lodz. But you didn't see such a masses. Singles there you hear, here and there. You know, oh my father [NON-ENGLISH]. My [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, so that's all what you heard. But when the Germans came in with-- this I remember. It stays in front of me-- with the motorcycles. And the guy sits on the other side here, and with the rifles here, the machine guns, this, and this, and this. And all the goyim, clapping and flowers thrown on them. And we stood down there. And not even a day, they start beating us up, breaking all the stores, breaking everything. You were afraid to go out even, afraid to go out. And the little stinkers like this, they wore a swastik already. Next day, swastik, Polacks, swastik. And they used to say, boy, oh boy. We going to pay you now. And they broke the glasses in the stores and the doors, and you are afraid to go out. You beaten-- they beat you up, little stinkers. The boy with sticks can hit with the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was afraid with the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was murder already. And you knew this is the problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of people start walking. You couldn't go on the train. You couldn't go on a bus, nothing. What did you do the first-- when they first came in, you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, we couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go out of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were in the house. I was going out, looking around this, and run back. Because you, you didn't have a way to buy a piece of bread already. They burned everything. They loot everything. They, they destroyed everything. There was-- the, the bakers they were blocking everything with iron, with this and that to bake a bread or something. And this would happen. And we saw trouble. Thousands of people were walking towards Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't? Your family didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So my brother, my brother, they, they took him to the army when the war broke out. And the one behind him-- I don't know, two years different or whatever-- he went with him. And my mother said it's OK. [NON-ENGLISH], you know, go save yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went close to-- not far already from Russia. And he changed his mind, the younger one. He said, no. I'm not going. What am I going to do there? Where? So he said, do what you want. My brother, he knew the Germans. He was in the army there in the war. And he smuggled out. They, they caught him. He, he had blond hair and blue eyes, and he looked like a goy. And he speak better Polish than the Polack, intelligent Polish. You have two types of Polish, you know, like country or, or real clear and nice. So he said, no. I know them. This is [NON-ENGLISH]. They're going to kill every Jew. He goes. So he went across. He went to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Russia and survived in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other one came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were laws instituted, all the laws about wearing [NON-ENGLISH], [INTERPOSING VOICES] and what laws were there for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think in '41 this happened that we start wearing a band or a [NON-ENGLISH] here. They, they vote out for, for the president-- not the Jewish made. This, you know, it's a funny thing. You know, you had a lot of rich people in the ghetto, Jewish people. One rich woman was my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --aunt. Rich, rich, rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto set up?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you enter-- how-- when did you go into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- the ghetto came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They poked-- they picked a, a poor neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they came out with the law about the ghetto? In your house, did you have to take people in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Everybody lived where they were. But there was plenty empty. Because the Polacks, they went out from the ghetto. So they had plenty of space for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what changed for you in the ghetto when you, when you were part of the Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could go to work. Didn't have a school, didn't have anything. And for a while, you know, little by little you didn't have what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, oh, a city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many blocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge, huge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Huge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the Judenrat in Lodz was rather well-known. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rumkowski was the head of Judenrat. Did you have any dealings with Rumkowski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You or your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was going in a nice, beautiful horse and a buggy, you know, a closed one, nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: A carriage, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A carriage, and this was he's driving in it. But this idiot, he had the Magen David here, too. And he was a son of a bitch, excuse me. A bitch. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and the Sonderkommando, you know, the police, this was our whole underworld.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Sonderkommando, are you talking about the Jewish Police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was it an underworld?

speaker: interviewee
text: The underworld, therefore crooks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The, the people from the, from-- they killed the, the cows, the animals to eat. They were brutal. People, they had no other money, no heart, you know. Those people, and this was a clique. And those people used to be the policemen. And they worked together. They thought maybe they're going to be better than-- treated by the Germans than those poor people or average person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were tough. They were tough. That didn't work. Well, I don't know if you are interesting in this right now. But, you know, this brings me back to see what people can do, like I was-- my brother died, and I came. Your brother died in--

speaker: interviewee
text: When he, when he died, but this was already--

speaker: interviewer
text: In, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: --later in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is later. Everybody, whatever who died is in the ghetto, outside not. Oh, we're young. So what happen is when this brother, my brother died, and I went to work for him, I, I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brother do in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, was no work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did he work outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, this was-- this was a part in the ghetto that the, the, the train, the cargo that used to bring in the food, the little food what came, or coal or wood, came in over there. They unload it, and they rationed this. Each time everybody got this and this and this and this--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: A pound of potatoes or, or a little coal or a little-- some bread, whatever they delivered, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he used to-- not just he-- 100 people, 200 people, it was big. You know? And he used to unload. Two people took a whole cargo, one, one, one-- what do you call this--

speaker: interviewer
text: A train. A car?

speaker: interviewee
text: A car. So he used to be good, and they liked him. So he used to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He got the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go to Rumkowski to get the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Probably it was the leaders from there. There was a head of it. I remember what his name was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We killed him in Rome after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this a German or a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew. We killed this guy in Rome, Lederman-Lederman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the head of it over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The head of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unloading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Head of unloading.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did he answer to? Did he have a boss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Germans. And he had nothing to do with the Judenrat or the Sonderkommando? He had nothing to do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm sure he, he knew everything, because he was a big, big man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the food for the Germans, too, or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Just Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Garbage, garbage, what came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Garbage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother worked there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the station that had got unloaded, in the ghetto or outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was apart from the ghetto, but was a big place where the train goes in. They, they, they moved everything, download it, till they, they spread this in the stores where they have to supply for the people, the Jewish. So it was a nice size of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother got sick, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He came home one day, and he said he has trouble with the knee. And he couldn't walk. So my sister says-- my mother was already very sad-- very, very, very like done. She saw, like, an end to it, you know, the whole thing in '43. You know, so she saw an end to it. And so she was not that, you know, effective, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister says, you know, Melech, come on, let's go to the hospital, some hospital. So they took him to the hospital. And she left him there, and that's it. They just died there. People [INAUDIBLE]. You never visited him in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't even know where this is and was nothing. You're [INAUDIBLE] in like an animal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took his, his position?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there, and I said I'm Melech's brother, a Jewish guy, a Jewish guy. Not this head. No, it was another two, two people that were lower that-- closer to the people, go do this, do that. The big shot was-- in the beginning I thought he's not Jewish even, this big guy with the boots, you know, high. I remember here, nice, nice, Polish military boots with the pants like this, you know, white here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we finished this guy in, in Rome. How long did you work in the unloading and loading there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked quite a long time. I don't know-- eight, nine, 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that help your family in food and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- if you want to hear a nice little story, too-- you might have forgot all about it. Now you say, in this place where I unload-- so I knew my brother, they used to give him every month about four or five days to go in a bakery, to work in a bakery. Over there, you get a piece of bread, enough to eat. So it was no work. So he went there, and he had bread enough to eat. And he used to steal home the bread, some bread, hide. In the bakeries, they used to count the bread. The Sonderkommando, those police, they were sitting two guys that had the keys to the flour. And they count the bread, how much bread goes into the oven, how much bread comes out from the oven that nobody steals and gets the bread. So now I remember, you know, it was a long hallway, long, the whole length of the bakery. One room was-- I don't know-- offices. And then when there was a table on the side, the two police were sitting always on this side. Here was a little room that the workers were sitting and eating. And then another, and the whole length, and then the end over there it was a tank with water to making the flour, the whole thing. And the next room is where they're putting in, in this pot the machine that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mixes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --turns the flour and makes the dough and, and mixes over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to send me. So I got the first time they sent me, I was good. Send me for a week, five days, go in the bakery, vacation. So I worked in the bakery. I know that my-- everybody is laying there dead, no food. And here is so much bread. My father was a, a finger, toothpick, a toothpick. He couldn't even move the, already the, the jaw. Dead people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the brother that came back, he didn't want to go. So he was laying in this side of the bed. He was laying in this side, dead people, dead. Nothing to do, nothing to eat. And my mother, nothing. Nothing, kids, nobody had to eat, nothing. Nothing to eat, really nothing-- a week, two weeks. Nothing. You eat grass. You pick grass. You did. You-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I, I was crazy in this bakery. So what I did, so I used to go and when the dough-- they made the dough, the machines-- nobody were there. Just [INAUDIBLE] you know? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to go and rip a piece from this one. To the next one, where the fire is burning from the wood to cook this. And I used to put the coal on the side. And I used to put this in. If they ever seen me, they would give me a bullet in the head outside. But I took a chance. I didn't think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were all people, Rumkowski people, the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumkowski people, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you, did you have any personal trouble or incidents with Mr. Rumkowski?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him in the streets a few times going back and forth. I saw him, I saw him coming to Auschwitz. And, and, and then behind him, and the Germans they were nice. Who is your relatives? Oh, everybody, everybody had a whole bunch sticking to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he came--

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Auschwitz with him or before him or after him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, oh, he came back later, later, later, when I was there already, when they closed the ghetto, when they closed the ghetto already. I don't know what-- which transport this was, after me. But they finished the ghetto and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah. Mr.-- did-- could you bribe Rumkowski if--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were young. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not just me, even other people. This Rumkowski had just met the, the, the, the [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call them, the top of it, you know, the top. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people get favors from Rumkowski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people, maybe. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah. You don't know about that. Were, were there-- were you in a [NON-ENGLISH] or an [NON-ENGLISH] in the ghetto when they took people out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They, they they came, the [INAUDIBLE]. They, they, they closed off the building. I don't remember if another building. But they closed everything. And everybody raus, raus, raus, raus. And then they went in to check. And they said take a pot. Take a fork and a spoon. You're going to work. And that's all what they said.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hide anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. My mother put the keys here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the house. What she hided, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Your father died in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother died in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the [NON-ENGLISH] came to go, to go, to leave the ghetto, there was just your mother and the little brothers, the rest of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go back to what happened [SIGHS] with your father and your brother. When somebody died in the ghetto, how were they buried? Was there a funeral?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people what they did to get their portion bread every week, and many times you didn't get it either. Because I came to the store. You stood from 2 o'clock at night by the window outside, cold or warm, whatever. And then they say, ha, no more. You always hope that you make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, this, this what happened. The, the, the kids, the kids standing-- I'm lost here. My question was what happened when somebody died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so what-- I have so many-- so many things in my head comes back. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If somebody died in the ghetto, there was nobody to take you, nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father or my brother or the other, what happened with him is they come with a wagon, and people pushed it. This was a [NON-ENGLISH], a [NON-ENGLISH]. People used to leave it outside on the sidewalk. People used to hide this. If you want-- if you lived in the, the beginning--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the buildings, and they had a hall for cold winter, to have some food fresh, they had halls. What you call this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cellars.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], cellars, right. Thank you. And, and they used to hide them to get the ration, the bread. You have a card.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you kept--

speaker: interviewee
text: And each time they ripped. So I had it. So I get more bread. So if they find out, the police, they beat you to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your father buried anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know. And your brother also, not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where. They filled up with 10 people, 20 people, whatever they can push.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Also was the-- did the ghetto have a gate? Could you go in and out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The ghetto was built. They closed it. And I don't remember exactly. They closed the ghetto in 1940. It took them almost-- since they, they taking over-- they took over the ghetto, they, they built the ghetto, put concrete poles and everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: Around the buildings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around it, and when they finished it, they finished at the end of '44, '40, '40. And they put electric in, high power. You touched it, you're gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside they had tall--

speaker: interviewer
text: Towers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, towers around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jewish police in any way involved in guarding the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They supposed to keep the, the, the, the order in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Order. Were they able to keep the order?

speaker: interviewee
text: They dealt with dead people. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you mentioned that they were mafia and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of them helpful at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe what they knew, maybe relatives. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you saw them as an animal, as a wild person.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think that this is even a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They act even like not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you speak in Yiddish-- in Polish for? They're so good to you? But they speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you, you left, what did-- I know that people were hungry, and they were sick, and they were dying. But was there any kind of other kind of life going on? There were children there. Did anybody get married? Were children born in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you heard is songs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people sitting with the feet on the side-- on the sidewalk and singing songs. You never heard beautiful songs, making songs. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was a, was a song one time in the camp or two, this we used to sing with a, a woman. You work a whole day, and you come and you-- they-- you wait. They give you outside a little soup, you know, for the day. And, and she takes us in the hand and looks and she has nothing to eat. It's a little water. So she sings a song. [NON-ENGLISH], go a little deeper, and get [NON-ENGLISH]. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thicker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thicker stuff a little bit inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the melody?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] You know, [LAUGHS] this was the song and another few words there. But this is what was. You know? You turned in plain as an animal. You see the dog goes, he smells on the floor. Oh, you find something. And these were the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to change our tape again.
